result:
The vast expanse of the cosmos beckons, a tapestry of stars woven from the Big Bang's fiery breath 13.8 billion years ago. Imagine hurtling through the Orion Arm of the Milky Way, our spiral galaxy home to 100-400 billion suns, hurtling at 828,000 km/h around its supermassive black hole Sagittarius A*, a beast 4 million times the Sun's mass.

Picture exoplanets: Proxima Centauri b, orbiting the closest star at 4.24 light-years, potentially rocky and in the habitable zone, where liquid water might dance under a dim red dwarf's glow. Or TRAPPIST-1's seven Earth-sized worlds, 40 light-years away, three possibly lush with oceans, their skies painted by sibling stars.

Black holes warp reality—Event Horizon Telescope's 2019 image of M87*'s shadow confirmed Einstein's general relativity, spacetime bending like a trampoline under a bowling ball. Hawking radiation whispers of their slow evaporation, quantum foam bubbling at the edge.

Life? Extremophiles on Earth thrive in boiling vents, acidic pools, Antarctic ice—hinting at Martian subsurface microbes or Europan oceans beneath cracked ice, warmed by tidal flexing from Jupiter's gravity.

Wormholes? Theoretical shortcuts through spacetime, stabilized by exotic matter with negative energy density. Faster-than-light? No, but Alcubierre's warp drive warps space itself, contracting ahead, expanding behind—needing energy of a star, but math checks out.

Humanity's shot: Voyager 1, 46 billion km out, carries the Golden Record—Bach, Chuck Berry, whale songs—for any cosmic neighbors. We're stardust, Carl Sagan said, pondering our pale blue dot. The universe expands, dark energy accelerating it to heat death in 10^100 years. But tonight, gaze up: infinity awaits. (248 words)
